source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession planting guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension small-farm planting calendars; UF/IFAS Extension Florida vegetable production handbook; UC ANR Master Gardener succession planting publications; SARE Cover Crops field guide; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower (succession-planting tables). Frost-anchored windows use last-spring-frost (LSF) as day 0; negative days fall before LSF and positive days after. Climate notes flag the climate types where the base window materially shifts; absence of a climate note means the base window is workable.

climateNotes.maritime: Season extends comfortably through June; keep sowing every 2 weeks until daytime highs cross 75F.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Bolts by mid-May; stop spring sowings around day +21 and switch to phase 2 early.
climateNotes.arid: Shade cloth (30 percent) extends the spring window by 2-3 weeks into early summer.

chains.notes: Choose loose-leaf and Batavian types for the most heat tolerance late in the spring window.
Heat-tolerant climbing substitute when lettuce bolts; one planting on a trellis fills the summer salad gap.

climateNotes.maritime: Carries into November under a low tunnel.
climateNotes.continental: Stop sowings 30 days before first fall frost; cold-hardy varieties (Winter Density, Rouge d'Hiver) extend by 2 weeks under row cover.

chains.notes: Fall succession; pick cold-hardy varieties as the window closes.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Romaine takes longer than leaf lettuce; space sowings 3 weeks to avoid harvest pileups.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Best fall crop; sow weekly here, the cool season is short.

chains.notes: Fall romaine often outperforms spring romaine in flavor and storage life.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR lettuce production guide

chains.notes: Butterheads bolt fast in heat; transplant 4-week starts every 2 weeks for steady heads.

climateNotes.maritime: Mid-September transplants size up under cool fall nights for late-October harvest.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window is narrow; stop by day +7 to avoid bolting.

chains.notes: Cold-tolerant; first sowings can go in as soon as soil works.
Heat-tolerant cooking-greens substitute; single planting after soil reaches 60F.

climateNotes.continental: Bloomsdale and Tyee overwinter under straw mulch in zone 5 and warmer.

chains.notes: Fall spinach is the best spinach; pick savoyed types for cold tolerance.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower

chains.notes: Cut at 4 inches before flower stalks elongate.

climateNotes.maritime: Holds in the ground through December under a low tunnel.

chains.notes: Summer gap is intentional; arugula goes bitter and flowers in heat above 80F.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Cut-and-come-again; harvest at 5 inches and the crown regrows twice.
Most cold-tolerant of the brassica greens; sweet after a frost.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Best fall crop; spring sowings bolt before heading.

chains.notes: Use small head varieties (Toy Choi) for tighter spacing.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds Asian greens guide; UF/IFAS Extension

chains.notes: Single spring planting carries through summer when picked weekly from the outside in.

climateNotes.maritime: Often overwinters; cut back hard in February and the same plant resumes.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall planting is the main crop; spring bolts by June.

chains.notes: Second sowing for fall and overwintering.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower

chains.notes: Spring planting; harvest from the bottom up so the crown keeps growing.

climateNotes.continental: Frost-sweetened fall kale is the prize crop; sow by mid-July for zone 5.
climateNotes.maritime: Overwinters reliably under a low tunnel.

chains.notes: Fall storage kale; lacinato holds quality longest in the ground.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Spring sowings; ready in 25-30 days. Stop when daytime highs cross 75F.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; summer sowings split and turn woody.

chains.notes: Fall sowings size up slower and store longer.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Keep seedbed evenly moist for 14 days; spotty germination is the main carrot succession failure.

climateNotes.continental: Fall carrots store best in the ground under straw; harvest as needed through January in zone 5.
climateNotes.arid: Mulch heavily to keep soil temperature below 85F for germination.

chains.notes: Fall and storage crop.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

chains.notes: Soak seedballs 8 hours before sowing for faster, more even germination.
Fall beets are the storage crop; harvest at 2-3 inches for best texture.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Salad turnips (Hakurei) at 40 days; storage types (Purple Top) at 60 days.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; spring sowings bolt by May.

chains.notes: Fall and storage crop; sweetens after first light frost.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Sow late July through August for fall harvest into December.
climateNotes.continental: Plant 10-12 weeks before first hard freeze for full-size roots.

chains.notes: Daikons need cool nights to size up; spring plantings bolt before bulbing.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds Asian roots guide; WSU Extension

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Stop sowings 70 days before first fall frost; rust pressure rises late summer.
climateNotes.maritime: Last sowing by day +70; bean season is short here.

chains.notes: Wait for soil at 60F; rotting risk is high in cold wet soil. Sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous picking.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide

chains.notes: Spring peas; trellis at planting time. Plant as soon as soil works.
Pull spent pea vines, leave roots and nodules in place, replant the same bed to beans.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall peas reliable here; harvest into November.
climateNotes.continental: Fall peas only succeed where first frost is after October 15.

chains.notes: Fall pea on the same trellis; reuses the structure twice.

entries.source: Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Two or three sowings 2 weeks apart spread harvest; all-at-once pods are the default failure.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds edamame guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Spring sowings; first crop is the heaviest.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; spring is too brief.

chains.notes: Fall sowings for cool-season harvest.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Spring crop; transplant 4-6 week starts. Side shoots extend harvest 4-6 weeks past the main head.

climateNotes.continental: Fall broccoli is the better crop in zones 5-6; cool nights tighten heads.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall sowings only; spring is too short.

chains.notes: Fall direct sow when soil cools below 75F.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Early-season variety (Early Jersey Wakefield, Caraflex) for late spring harvest.
Mid-season variety; transplant when daytime highs drop below 80F.

climateNotes.continental: Plant 12 weeks before first hard freeze for storage size.

chains.notes: Storage variety (savoy or red drumhead); frost-sweetened.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

chains.notes: Spring crop; cauliflower bolts at heat above 80F, so timing is tight.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the only reliable season; spring almost always buttons.

chains.notes: Fall crop; the better cauliflower season in most climates.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: First sowing once soil is 65F. Trellis to extend bearing life.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Second planting outruns the first against downy mildew which arrives in late July.
climateNotes.maritime: Second sowing only succeeds in long-season pockets; usually skip.

chains.notes: Second planting takes over as the first declines from bacterial wilt or downy mildew. 3-4 weeks apart.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

chains.notes: First planting; squash vine borer typically takes plants by late July.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Second planting is essential against vine borer; first plants rarely make August.
climateNotes.maritime: Second planting often succeeds well into September.

chains.notes: Replacement planting around the time borers hit phase 1.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Crookneck and patty-pan together cover the early summer window.
Second planting carries through August into early fall.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Last sowing by day +60; basil sulks below 55F nights.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Downy mildew arrives mid-summer; later sowings outrun first-planting losses.

chains.notes: Pinch flower buds; second and third plantings step in as the first goes woody.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds basil guide

chains.notes: Bolts fast in long days and heat; 2-week succession is the only way to keep leaves coming.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; cilantro is a cool-season herb here.
climateNotes.arid: Use Calypso or Slow Bolt strains; even fall sowings bolt fast in low-humidity heat.

chains.notes: Fall succession; cool nights slow bolting.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cilantro guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: For leaf dill (fronds), succession every 3 weeks; for seed dill, single early planting that flowers in midsummer.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds herb guide

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Powdery mildew arrives in August; later sowings hold longer in bloom.

chains.notes: Two or three sowings keeps cut-flower production steady from July through frost. Pinch first stems at 8 inches to encourage branching.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Early planting blooms July through August.
Mid-season planting blooms September through frost; sulphureus types are more heat-tolerant.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; Xerces Society pollinator publications

climateNotes.continental: Last sowing by day +60 to bloom before first frost.
climateNotes.maritime: Late sowings struggle to ripen; stop by day +49.

chains.notes: Branching types (Autumn Beauty, Lemon Queen) flower continuously off a single plant; single-stem types (ProCut, Sunrich) need 2-week succession for continuous blooms.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Fall-sown the previous September; overwinters and fixes 70-130 lb N per acre by spring termination.
Roll-crimp the clover at full bloom 2 weeks before transplanting; tomatoes go through the mulch into stored N.

climateNotes.continental: Sow by day +160 for adequate fall establishment in zones 5-6.

chains.notes: After tomato harvest, pull plants and overseed winter rye for next year's biomass and erosion control.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Rodale Institute farming systems trial

chains.notes: Fast warm-season smother crop; flowers at 5-6 weeks. Mow at first bloom before seed sets.
Fall brassica transplanted into the rolled buckwheat residue; weed-free bed and freed-up phosphorus.

climateNotes.continental: Sow by day +150 in zones 5-6; vetch needs 6 weeks of growth before hard freeze.

chains.notes: Overseeded between brassica rows in fall; overwinters and fixes 100-150 lb N per acre by spring.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Fall-sown legume cover; overwinters in zones 6 and warmer, fixes 90-150 lb N per acre by spring termination.
Heavy nitrogen feeder placed after the winter pea; takes up the freed-up N over the season.

climateNotes.maritime: Frost-seed in February instead, the cool wet spring establishes the clover well.
climateNotes.continental: Overseed at last cultivation around day +70 in zones 5-6 for fall establishment.

chains.notes: Overseeded into the corn between rows at last cultivation; provides ground cover after corn harvest and overwinters for next year.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Penn State Extension
